coredata.dc:title: Dynamic visualization monitoring of cell membrane damage using polarity-responsive amphiphilic carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Meng
Yang, Biwen
Tang, Jiayi
Ning, Mengling
Guan, Zerong
Li, Zhenzhen
Ye, Binggang
Zhong, Huiqing
Guo, Zhouyi
Liu, Zhiming
coredata.dc:description: Development of organelle-targeted fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has attracted extensive attention recently, however, the application of CDs for monitoring cell membrane integrity is rarely reported. Herein, we propose a kind of amphiphilic CDs (∼2.84 nm) derived from o-phenylenediamine and urea (termed M-CDs). M-CDs display high-affinity capacity to polysaccharide structures, which allows the rapid (∼within 30 s), wash-free fluorescence (FL) imaging of microbes, including bacteria and fungi. For mammalian cells, M-CDs can selective bind to the intracellular cytoskeletal filaments when the cells undergo cytomembrane destruction. More interestingly, the FL feature of M-CDs is polarity-dependent that emits the maximum emission wavelength from 501 to 561 nm when the solvent polarity increases from 0.02 to 0.32, which offers the opportunity to detect the polarity microenvironment. We observe that the polarity increases significantly when M-CDs pass through the broken cytomembrane. M-CDs are finally adopted for the fast morphometric analysis of abnormal erythrocytes in haematuria, providing a novel avenue for the rapid test of membrane-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic carbon dots
Polarity
Membrane damage
Rapid imaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FL microscope imaging based on M-CDs
Detection of plasma membrane damage on cells using M-CDs
Preparation and structure characterization of M-CDs
Rapid bioimaging using M-CDs as polarity-sensitive nanoprobe
Plasma membrane integrity monitoring based on polarity-responsive M-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32071399 and 62175071 ), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2021A1515011988 ), the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou ( 2019050001 ), the Project on the Education Department of Guangdong ( 2021KTSCX238 and 2022ZDZX2077 ), and the Natural Science Research Project of Guangdong Food and Drug Vocational College ( 2020ZR01 and 2022ZR02 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Low-magnification, (b) high-resolution TEM images, and (c) corresponding diameter distribution of M−CDs. (d) UV-−-vis spectrum of M-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of M-CDs. (f) Full scan XPS spectrum, and high-resolution (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s, (i) O 1 s spectra of M-CDs.
The 1H-NMR (a) and 13C-HMR (b) spectra of M-CDs. (c) Possible solvothermal reaction mechanism for the formation of M-CDs.

(a) FL emission spectra of M-CDs at various excitation wavelengths. (b) 3D FL spectroscopy analysis of M-CDs. (c) The FL decay curves of M-CDs dispersed in different solvents. (d) Photographs of M-CDs in various solvents under UV light excitation. (e) Normalized emission spectra of M-CDs in various solvents. (f) Photographs of M-CDs in dioxane solution containing different proportions of water H2O under a 365 nm UV lamp, and (g) corresponding FL emission spectra. (h) Linearity of the maximum emission wavelength, maximum emission FL intensity, and the solvent’s Δƒ. (i) FL spectra of M-CDs in methanol-glycerol mixtures with different viscosities.

(a) FL microscopy images of live E. coli, S. aureus, S. cerevisiae cells using M-CDs (100 μg/mL) as the FL nanoprobe. (b) FL microscopy images of HepG2 cells treated with M-CDs (100 μg/mL) for different times. (c) Illustration of the fast and wash-free imaging of S. cerevisiae cells based on M-CDs. FL images (d) and (e) corresponding FL intensity curve of S. cerevisiae cells incubated with 100 μg/mL M-CDs for 0-60 s and emission intensity changes at various time points. (f) Colocalization FL microscopy images of M-CDs (100 μg/mL) and Dil (15 μM) in S. cerevisiae cells. FL microscopy images of various cells treated with M-CDs (g) and commercial Dil dye (h), respectively.

(a) FL microscopy images of damage E. coli, S. aureus, S. cerevisiae cells treated with 0.1% benzalkonium bromide using M-CDs as the nanoprobe. (b) Classification of live and damaged S. cerevisiae cells using PCA analysis according to the PL color characteristics.

(a) FL intensities of M-CDs solutions mixed with different concentrations of lipopolysaccharide, mannan and chitin within 30 min, respectively. (b) FL intensity change of M-CDs treated with ergosterol, cholesterol or lecithin within 30 min. F: the FL intensity of M-CDs treated with different components. F0: the FL intensity of only M-CDs. (c, d) Illustrations of the targeting mechanisms of M-CDs to bacteria, fungi and damaged mammalian cells. (e) FL imaging of blood cells and shadows stained with M-CDs. (f) Raman analysis of the content change of proteins in erythrocytes after different treatments.

(a) Photographs of the agar plates treated with different drugs. (b) FL microscopy images of S. cerevisiae after treatment of different drugs. (c) FL imaging of HepG2 cells treated with different concentrations of TritonX-100 then stained with M-CDs. (d) Flow chart for imaging of hematuria samples. (e) FL microscopy images of abnormal erythrocytes in hematuria samples (Normal erythrocytes are taken from venous blood).
Synthesis of M-CDs and their applications for rapid bioimaging and membrane damage monitoring.

head.ce:title: Dynamic visualization monitoring of cell membrane damage using polarity-responsive amphiphilic carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & SATCM Third Grade Laboratory of Chinese Medicine and Photonics Technology, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Microecological Resources and Utilization in Breeding Industry, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Guangdong Haid Group Co., Ltd., Guangzhou 511400, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Microecological Resources and Utilization in Breeding Industry
Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Microecological Resources and Utilization in Breeding Industry, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Guangdong Haid Group Co., Ltd., Guangzhou 511400, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Healthy Medical Engineering Technology Research Center, Guangdong Food and Drug Vocational College, Guangzhou 510520, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Healthy Medical Engineering Technology Research Center
Guangdong Food and Drug Vocational College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Healthy Medical Engineering Technology Research Center, Guangdong Food and Drug Vocational College, Guangzhou 510520, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of organelle-targeted fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has attracted extensive attention recently, however, the application of CDs for monitoring cell membrane integrity is rarely reported. Herein, we propose a kind of amphiphilic CDs (∼2.84 nm) derived from o-phenylenediamine and urea (termed M-CDs). M-CDs display high-affinity capacity to polysaccharide structures, which allows the rapid (∼within 30 s), wash-free fluorescence (FL) imaging of microbes, including bacteria and fungi. For mammalian cells, M-CDs can selective bind to the intracellular cytoskeletal filaments when the cells undergo cytomembrane destruction. More interestingly, the FL feature of M-CDs is polarity-dependent that emits the maximum emission wavelength from 501 to 561 nm when the solvent polarity increases from 0.02 to 0.32, which offers the opportunity to detect the polarity microenvironment. We observe that the polarity increases significantly when M-CDs pass through the broken cytomembrane. M-CDs are finally adopted for the fast morphometric analysis of abnormal erythrocytes in haematuria, providing a novel avenue for the rapid test of membrane-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), the novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, have gained extensive attention for bioimaging. Due to their diverse physicochemical properties, good biocompatibility, low cost, simple preparation, and unique optical properties, such as tunable photoluminescence emission and high photostability [1–3]. Recently, CDs have been developed as promising organelle-targeted fluorescent nanoprobes for cell research. These include the nucleus/nucleolus, mitochondria, lysosomes, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lipid droplets, and more [4–15]. For example, Yang's group reported the modified CDs carrying nuclear localization signal peptides (PKKKRKVG) for nucleus imaging [16]. Without any tedious post-modification of ligands, Tong et al. synthesized CDs via a one-step hydrothermal treatment of rose bengal and branched polyethylenimine, which could target lysosomes because of the acidophilic effect of the amino groups on the CDs surface [8]. CDs achieve subcellular structure orientation through their inherent chemical compositions or modified surface ligands, as well as their surface chemical properties.

The cell membrane, as the outermost protective layer of the cell, plays important roles in various cellular physiological functions, such as cell proliferation, communication, and signal transduction. The integrity of the plasma membrane is crucial for cellular survival and directly reflects the pathological state of the body [17–19]. Currently, fluorescence (FL) imaging is the most direct and effective method for observing cell membranes. The commercial membrane-targeted probes mainly consist of small organic molecule dyes, whose fluorescent signals are easily quenched [20,21]. Several groups have also developed new types of FL materials for membrane imaging [22–24], however, few have been reported for the dynamic monitoring of membrane damage. Furthermore, these fluorescent probes necessitate extended incubation periods for staining and laborious washing procedures, which can disrupt the cellular surroundings and lead to cell loss during the washing process. They also do not meet the criteria for continuous monitoring of biological processes [25,26].

Herein, a novel type of amphiphilic CDs with membrane-targeting capability (M-CDs) is synthesized using a one-pot solvothermal method and easily accessible precursors (Scheme 1 ). The surface of M-CDs contains various polar and nonpolar groups (such as N-H, –CH3, ketone group, and benzimidazole ring) that give them favorable amphiphilicity for anchoring to membranes. M-CDs can emit polarity-sensitive FL signals ranging from green to orange as the polarity of the medium increases, suggesting the potential for detecting cellular microenvironment polarity. For FL imaging, M-CDs can serve as a novel FL nanoprobe for rapid, wash-free morphological observation of microbes, including bacteria and fungi, due to their high affinity to polysaccharide structures. While M-CDs can only to the intracellular cytoskeletal filaments of broken mammalian cells. In light of this, we have finally developed a rapid and polarity-sensitive morphological FL test for abnormal erythrocytes in hematuria. To our knowledge, this is the first report on the use of polarity-sensitive CDs to visualize cell membrane damage by the phenomenon of discoloration.

All commercially available reagents were used directly without further purification. urea, o-phenylenediamine, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), formamid, ethyl acetate (EA), dioxane, acetone, acetonitrile (ACN), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, methanol, glycerol, petroleum ether (bp 60–90 ℃), lecithin, cholesterol, ergosterol, mannan, TritonX-100, Tween 20 were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Chitin was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Lipopolysaccharide was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). 10X PBS was purchased from Biosharp (Hefei, China). Benzalkonium chloride (50 % w/w aq. soln) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Lysogeny broth (LB) medium and yeast extract peptone dextrose (YPD) medium were purchased from Huankai Microbial Co., Ltd (Guangzhou, China).

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100F electron microscope (JEOL). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in the 400–4000 cm−1 range using a Bruker ALPHA FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained with a Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy was done on AVANCE NEO Bruker-600 MHz. FL and ultraviolet visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a F-320 FL spectrometer (GANGDONG, China) and a UV-6100 UV-visible spectrophotometer (MAPADA, China), respectively. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured with a Malvern Zeta Sizer Nano (Malvern Instruments). Fluorescent imaging studies were performed with a MF53 inverted FL microscope (Micro-shot Technology Co., Ltd, Guangzhou, China). Raman spectra were obtained from a confocal Raman spectroscopy (Invia, Renishaw, Derbyshire, England) equipped with a 514 nm laser.

M-CDs were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method. In detail, o-phenylenediamine (100 mg) and urea (67 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL DMF. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL ploy (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and autoclaved at 160 ℃ for 10 h. After the reactor was cooled down to room temperature automatically, the obtained solution was firstly purified via 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. Then, the unreacted solvent was removed by rotary evaporator, it was further purified via 4 h dialysis against water with a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500 Da), and finally freeze-dried to collect the powder.

Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were all cultured in a LB medium. Saccharomyces cerevisiae (S. cerevisiae) was cultured in a YPD medium. After incubation in a shaking incubator overnight, preliminary bacterial suspensions were obtained. Then, diluted the bacterial suspension to an appropriate multiple and reinoculated it into solid culture medium. HepG2 cells were incubated in DEME with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % antibiotics at 37 ℃ in a humidified atmosphere of 5 % CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL microscope imaging based on M-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under aseptic conditions, a droplet of sterile water was placed onto the pristine microscope slide. A small quantity of bacterial moss was picked from the solid medium and mixed with a droplet of sterile water to form a film. The bacterial suspension can be applied directly as a smear. M-CDs (100 μg/mL) were applied onto the desiccated smears and observed using a FL microscope without the requirement of washing. Meanwhile, as a colocation analysis material, Dil (15 μM) was used to costain the cells and image cell membrane. Dil was incubated with cells for 10 min and washed twice. Colocation analysis was completed by ImageJ software. For imaging HepG2 cells, the culture medium within the pore plate was aspirated, and a solution of M-CDs (100 μg/mL) was introduced for incubation at various time intervals in order to imaging.

In order to investigate what structural components of the cell membrane bind to M-CDs, varying amounts (0–1.25 mg/mL) of ergosterol, cholesterol, lecithin, mannan, chitin and lipopolysaccharide were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol and treated with M-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 30 min. The FL intensity of the samples was measured using a FL spectrophotometer.

In order to ascertain the interaction between M-CDs and skeletal filaments, the blood shadow was prepared. The erythrocytes were extracted from the blood sample of a volunteer, approved by the Special Scientific Research Ethics Committee of South China Normal University. 3.5 mL of whole blood was acquired and underwent centrifugation at a speed of 2500 rpm/min for 10 min, then washed twice with triple normal saline to isolate the erythrocytes. After removing the supernatant, a red blood cell suspension was prepared by adding 1:1 normal saline to the precipitated cells. 0.5 mL suspension of erythrocytes were dissolved immediately upon mixing with 7 mL pH 7.4, 10 mmol/L phosphate buffer, and then stored in the refrigerator (4 ℃) for 1 h to ensure complete hemolysis. Next, the sample was centrifuged at a low temperature of 4 ℃ and high speed (13000 g) for 40 min. The supernatant was carefully poured off, and the remaining pellet was washed triple with PBS. Repeated the centrifugation procedure to acquire a blood shadow. Finally, the blood shadow was treated with TritonX-100 and hypertonic saline respectively for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of plasma membrane damage on cells using M-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: S. cerevisiae cells were incubated with 0.1 % benzalkonium bromide, 0.1 % Tween 20, and 2.5 mg/mL amphotericin B for 30 min, respectively, and then filmed for observation. HepG2 cells were seeded into a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h. TritonX-100 lysis buffer (0, 10, 25, 50 μM) was applied to the cells for 10 min. Then, the cells were washed twice with PBS buffer. Next, dropped M-CDs (100 μg/mL) solution and imaged HepG2 cells under the FL microscope. Detection of damaged erythrocytes in the urine of patient with urinary occult blood, the collection of morning urine samples from a volunteer. The volunteer willingly participated in the study and obtained approval from the Special Scientific Research Ethics Committee of South China Normal University. The sample was centrifuged at 1500 r/min for 5 min. After discarding the supernatant, the resulting precipitate was spread onto a slide. A solution of M-CDs (100 μg/mL) was then applied for imaging by FL microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structure characterization of M-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: M-CDs were prepared via one-pot solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and urea as the precursors and DMF as the solvent at 160 °C for 10 h. As seen in Fig. 1 a-c, the morphology of M-CDs observed by TEM reveals that M-CDs display a spherical shape with an average size of ∼2.84 nm. M-CDs possess clear lattice fringe space of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite. The UV−vis spectrum of the M-CDs aqueous dispersion exhibits an absorption peak at ∼420 nm (Fig. 1d). FT-IR analysis was employed to characterize the functional groups of the M-CDs (Fig. 1e), which depicted several stretching vibrations associated with N-H (3408 cm−1), C-H (2941 cm−1), C=O (1741 cm−1), C=C/C=N (1600–1680 cm−1), C-N (1419 cm−1) [10,14]. XPS was further performed to understand the surface chemistry and valance states of M-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1f, the peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s dominate in the XPS pattern of M-CDs. In detail, three component peaks corresponding to sp2 carbon (C=C, 284.5 eV), sp3 carbon (C−N/C−O, 285.6 eV), and carbonyl groups (C=O, 288.7 eV) appear in the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1g) [7]. The two component peaks at 399.5 and 400.1 eV in N 1 s spectrum can be assigned to C-N-C and N-H, respectively (Fig. 1h). The two-component O 1 s peaks at 531.5 and 533.1 eV orginate from C=O and C−O, respectively (Fig. 1i) [8]. Therefore, we deduce that the M-CDs surface is rich in a large number of functional groups like N-H, –CH3, ketone group, and benzimidazole structures, which confers the amphiphilicity of M-CDs.

To accurately understand the chemical structure of M-CDs, NMR spectroscopy was further conducted, as shown in Fig. 2 . The 1H-NMR spectrum of M-CDs (Fig. 2a) presents several dominant peaks in 6.8–8.0 ppm, such as: 7.90 (dd, J 1=6.5, J 2=3.4 Hz, 2H), 7.56 (dd, J 1=6.5, J 2=3.4 Hz, 2H), 6.94 (t, J=0.2 Hz, 3H), 6.93 (s, 1H). The peaks at 7.20, 7.56, 7.59 and 7.90 ppm are assigned to aromatic structure. Compared the chemical shift at 7.56 and 7.90 ppm, the appearance of peaks at 7.20 and 7.59 ppm indicates that the product has more than one aromatic compounds. The irregular peaks at 6.94 and 6.93 ppm that may attribute from the H of the fused aromatic rings with edge groups [27,28]. Fig. 2b shows the 13C-NMR spectrum of M-CDs, where the peaks in the range of 100–140 ppm are attributed to aromatic ring carbon, while the C=O chemical shift appears at 155.86 ppm. Besides, two peaks at 2.75 ppm (Figure S1) and 56.59 ppm in the 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectra respectively can be assigned to the methyl group [29]. Based on above, a possible formation mechanism of M-CDs is proposed (Fig. 2c). At high temperature condition (160 °C), o-phenylenediamine may react with urea to form 2(3H)-benzimidazolone. Schiff base reaction may also take place between the amino group of o-phenylenediamine and the aldehyde group of DMF during solvothermal treatment, leading to the formation of N, N-dimethyl-1H-benzo[d]imidazol-2-amine after undergoing dehydrogenation. However, due to the weak intensity of the H on the methyl in 1H-NMR spectrum, we infer that the initially formed benzimidazole intermediate gradually loses its dimethylamino group due to instability, resulting in the generation of 1(H)-benzo[d]imidazole [30,31]. With the increase of temperature and the extension of reaction time, the molecule grows and carbonizes, further forms fused aromatic rings by edge connection, and finally stacks to form spherical M-CDs through π–π interaction [27,32].

Fig. 3 a and b display the photoluminescence spectra and corresponding three-dimensional (3D) FL map of M-CDs, respectively, revealing an excitation-independent FL emission; and the optimal excitation wavelength centers at about 450 nm. The photoluminescence decay curves of M-CDs in different solvents are presented in Fig. 3c, which can be well fitted by a monoexponential decay, and the corresponding FL quantum lifetimes are calculated to be 4.38, 3.25, 3.00, 2.93, 1.90 ns, respectively, revealing that the radiative decay process is extremely pure with a single emissive center. We also observe that M-CDs display remarkable positive solvatochromic properties on their emission in different solvents (Fig. 3d). The maximum emission wavelength redshifts (from 418 nm to 566 nm) as the solvent polarity increases (Fig. 3e). M-CDs show stable green emission in most organic solvents, but the FL redshift when dispersed in polar proton solvents (ethanol and water), which probably due to the C=N bond makes fluorescent groups on the surface of M-CDs produce push and pull electrons, exhibiting an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect [30,33].

To determine the sensitivity of M-CDs to polarity, the FL spectra of M-CDs were measured in dioxane/H2O mixtures with different water content (ƒ w). As shown in Fig. 3f, the M-CDs solution changes color significantly from green to orange with ƒ w increases. When the solvent changes from dioxane to water, the FL intensity of M-CDs reduces and the maximum emission wavelength redshift from 501 nm to 561 nm (Fig. 3g). Evidently, take the polarity parameter (Δƒ)=0.262 as the dividing point, M-CDs show a linear correlation between fluorescent intensity and solvent polarity. At the same time, the maximum emission wavelength of M-CDs has a good linear response to the polarity from 30 % water (Δƒ=0.277) to 100 % water (Δƒ=0.32) (Fig. 3h). The superior polarity sensitivity and linearity allows M-CDs to be acutely aware of changes in the physiological environment surrounding the cell through FL discoloration. In addition, the FL responses of M-CDs to different viscosities were explored in a series of methanol-glycerol mixtures with different volume proportions. As shown in Fig. 3i, from methanol (0.59 cp) to 99 % glycerol (945 cp), the viscosity of the system increases by more than 1000 times; however, the FL intensity variation of M-CDs does not exceed 1 time. Therefore, it can be concluded that M-CDs are not affected by viscosity. This implies that M-CDs have excellent anti-interference capabilities in complex biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid bioimaging using M-CDs as polarity-sensitive nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: We then investigated the FL bioimaging capability of M-CDs for different types of cells, including bacteria, fungi, and mammalian cells. Fig. 4 a shows the FL images of Gram-negative (G-) E. coli, Gram-positive (G+) S. aureus, and S. cerevisiae cells after incubation with M-CDs for 5 min, where bright yellow-green FL signals around the cells are observed. While the HepG2 cells emit no FL under the same experimental condition (Fig. 4b). Only weak FL signals are noticed in the mammalian cells at 12 h, which can be ascribed to the intracellular permeation of M-CDs after the long-time incubation. We further conducted a fast FL imaging experiment using M-CDs as the nanoprobe without washing (Fig. 4c). As shown in Fig. 4d, bright FL images of S. cerevisiae cells can be clearly observed as early as 30 s and the emission intensity is rapidly stabilized within 1 min (Fig. 4e). We can also notice that M-CDs are almost dispersed on the cell surface which arouses the speculation about the membrane-targeting of M-CDs. To confirm this, S. cerevisiae cells were co-incubated with M-CDs and Dil (a membrane-targeting commercial fluorescent probe) for co-localization analysis. As demonstrated in Fig. 4f, the FL images treated with M-CDs and Dil almost completely overlap, with a Pearson’s co-localization coefficient calculated to be 0.9785, indicating that M-CDs possess a high specificity to the cell membrane. We can also note that the membrane-targeting effect of M-CDs is sustained even after a long-time observation for 140 min (Figure S2). In addition, we compared the staining effects of M-CDs and the commercial probe, as displayed in Fig. 4g and h, respectively, both of which illustrated distinct contours of four kinds of microbes, although the background FL is relatively obvious in Dil-treated biosamples. It can be noticed that the sample procedure for M-CDs (wash-free, 30 s) is considerably more facile than that for commercial probe (washing, 10 min), indicating the promising potential of M-CDs for point-of-care test of microbes.

We also detected damaged microbial cells treated with 0.1 % benzalkonium bromide using M-CDs as the FL nanoprobe. It can be observed that the damaged cells exhibit orange FL signals (Fig. 5 a), which is markedly different from the signals of live cells (Fig. 4a). We speculate that benzalkonium bromide alters bacterial cytoplasmic membrane permeability, leading to the leakage of cytoplasmic material and an increase in water content around the cell, ultimately enhancing the polarity of the microenvironment. Then, we transformed the FL colors of live and damaged cells into corresponding red (R), green (G), blue (B) color channels as three features for principal component analysis (PCA) classification. It can be observed that the live and damaged cells can be clearly distinguished based on the polarity-sensitive FL nanoprobe (Fig. 5b). Therefore, M-CDs switch on a novel way for the detection of live/damage cells through polarity change. In addition, M-CDs display excellent cytocompatibility (Figure S3), indicating the potential for bioapplications.

The discrepant FL imaging effects on microbial and mammalian cells implies M-CDs may perform different targeting mechanisms. We speculate that the rapid microbial imaging may be ascribed to the high-affinity of M-CDs to the polysaccharide structures that are rich on the surface of microbial cells. A confirmatory experiment was thus conducted according previous work [34,35]. It can be noted from Fig. 6 a that the FL intensities of M-CDs increase significantly after mixing with the polysaccharides (lipopolysaccharides, mannan and chitin), and the FL intensities are proportional to polysaccharide content. We deduce that the hydrophobic interaction forms between polysaccharide and the hydrophobic groups on M-CDs, leading to the enhanced FL intensity. We also detected the FL intensity of M-CDs in the presence of lipids (lecithin, cholesterol, or ergosterol), which changed slightly with the increase of lipid content (Fig. 6b), indicating a weak interaction. Finally, the models for the targeting mechanism of M-CDs to microbes are presented in Fig. 6c.

For mammalian cells with a surface lacking polysaccharide composition, M-CDs are unable to attach to the cell membrane immediately, resulting in faint FL images. However, as the incubation time increase, the FL signals emanate from the plasma membrane at 12 h and the FL color appears orange (Fig. 4b), which proposes a possibility of M-CDs being located inside the plasma membrane. As M-CDs contain benzimidazole groups that have been reported to have the affinity with microtubulin [36–39], we hypothesized that M-CDs entered into damaged mammalian cells and then attached to the skeleton fibers on the inner side of the plasma membrane (Fig. 6d). So we selected red blood cell, an ideal model for studying the membrane skeleton, to verify this. As demonstrated in Fig. 6e, intact red blood cells show no FL signal which is consistent with that of HepG2 cells (Fig. 4b). While the blood shadow with broken membrane is evidently illuminated with a bright halo around the around the cellular outline. Then the blood shadow was treated with TritonX-100, a detergent could remove some membrane proteins not decisive in cellular morphology [40], which had little effect on the FL image of blood shadow. The decrease of membrane proteins in blood shadow after treatment can also be confirmed by the Raman analysis (Fig. 6f). However, the FL signal almost disappeared when the blood shadow was treated with hypertonic saline that could lead to the denaturation of membrane skeleton proteins supporting cellular morphology [41]. These data confirm the assumption on the specific imaging of M-CDs on damaged cytomembrane via selective binding on the intracellular cytoskeletal filaments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plasma membrane integrity monitoring based on polarity-responsive M-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential of M-CDs for membrane damage detection, three agents were adopted to treat S. cerevisiae which could cause various degrees of membrane injury. Amphotericin B can bind to ergosterol on the surface of S. cerevisiae, producing an aggregate that creates a transmembrane channel that causes the leakage of cytoplasmic contents [42,43]. Benzalkonium bromide, a cationic surfactant, can alter cell membrane permeability and allow extravasation of cytoplasmic materials [44,45]. Tween 20 is a mild surfactant and does not dissolve the cytoplasmic membrane [46]. Fig. 7 a shows the colonies of S. cerevisiae after treatment with the three drugs at the same dose, where the antimicrobial effects are observed to be as follows: benzalkonium bromide > amphotericin B > Tween 20 (Figure S4). The corresponding FL images of S. cerevisiae cells are presented in Fig. 7b, where the FL color changes dominantly after treatment. The cells underwent benzalkonium bromide emit orange FL, which implies the most severe membrane damage. Interestingly, the FL after amphotericin B treatment had both orange and yellow colors, while the cells treated with surfactant only had one color. The uneven lethality of amphotericin B to S. cerevisiae cells may be ascribed to the binding mechanism of this antibiotic to ergosterol which is relatively more difficult than that of surfactants. We also conducted the FL imaging of HepG2 cells labeled with M-CDs after treatment with different concentrations of TritonX-100. It can be noticed from Fig. 7c that the cell morphology gradually becomes rounded and the cellular adhesion ability decreases, which can be due to the extent of cell membrane damage increases with the increasing concentration of TritonX-100. The FL color shifts from yellow-green to orange and corresponding FL intensity gradually rises with the TritonX-100 concentration increases, indicating the polarity change is closely related to the membrane damage.

In clinical practice, hematuria, characterized by the broken erythrocytes in urine, is a red flag that can be associated with urological malignancies, urinary tract infections, and kidney disease [47,48]. Morphological analysis of urine erythrocytes is a common method for diagnosing hematuria. However, it is difficult to recognize membrane abnormalities in erythrocytes using a bright field microscope. We explored the potential of FL technique using M-CDs as the nanoprobe. Mid-stream urine from a patient with nephritis and urinary occult blood was collected and stained with M-CDs for fluorescent microscopic observation (Fig. 7d). As shown in Fig. 7e, the abnormal erythrocytes collected from nephritis patient have various morphologies, which are significantly different from that from normal erythrocytes. However, we cannot observe whether the cell membrane is damaged from the bright field images. After being labeled with M-CDs, a flagrant contrast between normal and abnormal erythrocytes is noticed, where normal erythrocytes are invisible while the abnormal erythrocytes are illuminated with bright FL around the cytomembrane. The orange FL also unveils a significantly increased polarity microenvironment, reflecting a severe membrane destruction in nephritis-related abnormal erythrocytes. Notably, the notches of the moon-shaped erythrocytes can be rapidly labeled by M-CDs rather than labeling the entire erythrocyte, suggesting precise in situ labeling of M-CDs for the recognition of cellular damage. Therefore, M-CDs-based FL imaging technique provides a facile morphological and colorimetric method to assess the membrane integrity in clinics.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a novel amphiphilic CDs with membrane-targeting ability via a one-pot solvothermal method, and explored their applications for fast and wash-free FL bioimaging as well as the cell membrane integrity monitoring. The obtained M-CDs (∼2.84 nm) are highly crystalline with clear lattice fringe space of 0.21 nm. M-CDs possess several hydrophilic and hydrophobic functional groups determined by FT-IR, XPS and NMR analyses, which endow M-CDs with the amphiphilic property. The optimal excitation wavelength of M-CDs is about 450 nm, and emits bright yellow-green fluorescence. We notice that M-CDs display remarkable affinity to polysaccharide structures of microorganisms through hydrophobic interaction, which makes them serve as a robust tool for the rapid microbial morphology detection (within 30 s). Furthermore, when damage occurs in mammalian cells, the benzimidazole structure of M-CDs binds to cytoskeleton filaments, enabling a straightforward, fast, real-time assessment of membrane damage in space without the need for any specialized instrumentation. Most strikingly, the FL emission of M-CDs is polarity-dependent that shifts from 501 to 561 nm related to the solvent polarity from 0.02 to 0.32. The polarity dependency of M-CDs endows them with the promising potential for rapid morphometric analysis of erythrocytes in blood and urine, providing a unique new avenue for exploring polar microenvironments and offers a novel approach for the rapid detection of membrane-associated diseases.

Meng Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Biwen Yang: Project administration, Software, Supervision, Validation. Jiayi Tang: Investigation, Project administration, Supervision. Mengling Ning: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Zerong Guan: Methodology, Software. Zhenzhen Li: Data curation, Methodology. Binggang Ye: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision. Huiqing Zhong: Investigation, Supervision. Zhouyi Guo: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision. Zhiming Liu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.149038.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
